blocks.body: Linking Words shows you how to connect ideas through reason, result, contrast, and purpose so that your text sounds logical.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"Linking words help you show the logical connection between two ideas. They make a text not only correct, but also coherent.","rules":[{"label":"Reason","color":"emerald","text":"We explain why something happened with because, since, as.","example":"We left early because the weather was getting worse."},{"label":"Result and contrast","color":"blue","text":"We show result or contrast with words like so, therefore, however, although.","example":"It was late, so we took a taxi."},{"label":"Purpose","color":"amber","text":"We talk about purpose with to, in order to, so that.","example":"She wrote it down so that she wouldn't forget."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. The main families of linking words
blocks.body:
• Reason - why? because, since, as
  • Result - what happened next? so, therefore, as a result
  • Contrast - what is different or unexpected? although, though, however
  • Purpose - what is the aim? to, in order to, so that

Each family expresses its own logic, so the words are not always interchangeable.

blocks.heading: 2. Position in the sentence
blocks.body:
Some linking words introduce a clause, while others work more like a separate link between sentences.

  • because / although / so that usually introduce a subordinate clause.
  • however / therefore / as a result often stand at the beginning of a new sentence or after a semicolon.
  • so often joins two simpler ideas in one sentence.

blocks.heading: 3. Punctuation and style
blocks.body:
• Although and because do not need a comma before them when they come in the middle of the sentence: I stayed home because I was tired.
  • When the subordinate clause comes first, a comma is often needed: Although it was late, we kept working.
  • However and therefore often feel more formal than but or so.

blocks.heading: 4. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: using however as if it were an ordinary conjunction like but.
  • Mistake: confusing reason and result. Because explains the cause, while so shows the consequence.
  • Mistake: mixing purpose structures. to do and so that + clause do not work in the same way.

blocks.heading: 5. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• First choose the meaning: reason, result, contrast, or purpose.
  • Then check whether you need a word + clause pattern or a sentence adverb.
  • Punctuation often depends on the position of the linking word.
  • The same logic does not mean the same grammar, so learn the pattern together with the word.
